posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Jeremy’s Ramonet post made me thirsty. So I opened this—2010 Ruchottes. Gorgeous young wine with herbal notes, hints of Ramonet menthol, great drive and intensity with clean fresh citrus fruit. Nose is intense and beguiling. Super acidity. Great balance and length. Near grand cru level. Will open a 2004 Batard this weekend at Robuchon.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: finally figured how to turn the picture—edit it first.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: So you held back some from the ones you sold to me? Good to know it has come around!! Exactly what I was hoping for in my last bottle I’m saving!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I have some of the 14, anyone try any lately?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Great wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Do I misremember, or did you once express dislike for this wine? It is evolving well so far.

I think it’s terrific, the best Ruchottes between 08 and 14.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: mea culpa. Either a bad day tasting or an off bottle—I panned the wine. Either it’s better or I am. It was a great bottle—Buzz’ gain.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
mea culpa. Either a bad day tasting or an off bottle—I panned the wine. Either it’s better or I am. It was a great bottle—Buzz’ gain.

I remember! We crossed swords on the subject several years ago! Next time you make it to Burgundy we can drink a bottle or two to celebrate coming into alignment.

Crazy how undervalued the Ramonet 1er Crus were in the market until a year or tow ago. I guess a combination of premox blow-back, a bad importer, and not letting any journalists taste…

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked: This house knocked it out of the park in this year for whites, IMHO. I’m pretty sure I’ve also had one of these with similar shudderlicious reaction. The Montrachet was my WOTY a few years ago and a wine I honestly believed could go out 100 years if it remained free of the pox. I tend to love Ramonet for this plot anyway in most years.

posts.name: David Goerisch
posts.cooked: What’s the story with the different Ramonet labels - same wine, same vintage, some labels are white/blue and some are cream/green? There are examples on Google images for many different years dating back many years, so this doesn’t seem like a change over from one style to another.

posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
‘10 is gorgeous. Couldn’t keep my hands off and they’re all gone now.

Anyone have thoughts on ‘12? I opened a bottle few years ago and it was quite promising, of course far too young.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Crazy how undervalued the Ramonet 1er Crus were in the market until a year or tow ago. I guess a combination of premox blow-back, a bad importer, and not letting any journalists taste…

Premox- I have never ever had a premoxed bottle of Ramonet. I know the 2002 Ruchottes was a culprit, but outside of that, I’ve found the premox tag not applicable with Ramonet. I’m not sure if you’ve had the same experience, but certainly a tag that followed this producer.

I know you are a journalist, but I’m not sure that’s a reason why the wines were hard to find. The importer was/is dreadful.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
What’s the story with the different Ramonet labels - same wine, same vintage, some labels are white/blue and some are cream/green? There are examples on Google images for many different years dating back many years, so this doesn’t seem like a change over from one style to another.

The white labels were the euro market labels, and the green ones were the C&E labels. If I’m not mistaken, they are all the Euro labels now.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
I have some of the 14, anyone try any lately?

Maybe not helpful, but I just opened a 2013, and it was ridiculous. Tasted like BBM.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Crazy how undervalued the Ramonet 1er Crus were in the market until a year or tow ago. I guess a combination of premox blow-back, a bad importer, and not letting any journalists taste…

Premox- I have never ever had a premoxed bottle of Ramonet. I know the 2002 Ruchottes was a culprit, but outside of that, I’ve found the premox tag not applicable with Ramonet. I’m not sure if you’ve had the same experience, but certainly a tag that followed this producer.

I know you are a journalist, but I’m not sure that’s a reason why the wines were hard to find. The importer was/is dreadful.

Ramonet was hit early. The 95s and 96s, gorgeous on release, were and are a minefieid. 1996 Ruchottes had a high pox rate starting to my recollection in 1999 or 2000. (But no one called it premox yet to my knowledge. That term came later.).

There was some back and forth on this in a thread maybe 9 months to a year back.

posts.cooked:

I know you are a journalist, but I’m not sure that’s a reason why the wines were hard to find. The importer was/is dreadful.

There’s plenty of premoxed Ramonet out there. Had a bunch of oxidized and oxidizing 04 Bâtard earlier this year.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Crazy how undervalued the Ramonet 1er Crus were in the market until a year or tow ago. I guess a combination of premox blow-back, a bad importer, and not letting any journalists taste…

Ramonet was hit early. The 95s and 96s, gorgeous on release, were and are a minefieid. 1996 Ruchottes had a high pox rate starting to my recollection in 1999 or 2000. (But no one called it premox yet to my knowledge. That term came later.).

There was some back and forth on this in a thread maybe 9 months to a year back.

Funny you pick those wines. I have two clients that owned cases of the 1996 Ruchottes and 1999 Ruchottes, and both aged magically. I’m not sure what, if any, the correlation was, but they were the European market labels, and not the C&E labels. They also owned quite a few of the top wines too, and had zero bad bottles.
